title: My Presentation
cleaned_text: ... When I was 12 years old, I got raped by my uncle, and it was a really bad experience.mm Transcribe EVERYTHING spoken, exactly as spoken.
recommendations: Keep your pace exactly where it is (135 WPM is excellent) and use it to your advantage: add intentional “silent beats” instead of filler/hesitation sounds. Practice this drill: read a short paragraph aloud and, every time you feel an “um/uh” coming, stop and take one quiet breath (1–2 seconds) before continuing. Record yourself and aim to replace at least 3 of the 5 filler/hesitation moments with clean silence.

Reduce filler words and hesitation sounds (both ~17 per 100 words) by switching to a simple planning structure before you speak: use a 3-point outline (Point–Reason–Example). Before answering, take one breath and mentally label your next sentence as “Point.” This tiny pre-plan lowers uncertainty and makes you less likely to reach for “um/uh/like” while searching for words.

Your pauses are taking ~14% of speaking time; make them feel confident by placing them on purpose. Mark 2–3 pause spots in your next practice talk: (1) after your opening sentence, (2) before a key word, (3) after a key point. Hold each pause for a slow count of 1. This turns pauses from “stuck” moments into emphasis, which will lift your overall confidence score quickly.

Build confidence with a repeatable warm-up and a low-stakes repetition routine: 60 seconds of slow diaphragmatic breathing + 60 seconds of speaking on an easy topic (your day) while keeping your mouth moving and sentences short. Then repeat the same 30–45 second message 3 times, trying to cut filler/hesitations each round (5 → 3 → 1). Track the count—seeing it drop is a fast confidence booster.
